narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.5% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 37, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, family income in the middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. The model weighed these factors against patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on what best predicts bar success without considering gender or race. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1, which stands well below the average of about 6 for those who passed, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 37 pulled in the opposite direction, slightly favoring a pass prediction since it's close to the 37.27 average among successful students, showing the model saw real strength there. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 had a milder positive effect toward passing, though it trailed the 3.24 average for bar passers, tempering the overall outlook. Compared to typical admitted students—who often have higher decile rankings, especially later in school, and stronger GPAs—your profile showed promise in some areas like your LSAT but was held back by that Year 3 performance, placing you outside the safer zone for predicted success. Your middle family income aligns with over a third of passers, and full-time status matches nearly all of them, so those didn't sway things much. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting where risks appeared highest based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or strengthening your later academic deciles through targeted prep or additional coursework, which could shift future predictions in your favor. Many students in similar spots improve by focusing on these levers and reapplying, often successfully. We encourage you to seek feedback from advisors and explore programs with bar passage support tailored to full-time students like you. Your path isn't closed—it's just asking for a strategic next step.
